insights: Zero incidents were assigned during any agent's declared leave periods, confirming strict adherence to scheduling constraints and eliminating the risk of operational disruption from misassignment.
Incident assignments are perfectly balanced across all five agents (100 incidents each), with no concentration, backlog, or disparity in workload distribution.
All 500 incidents were assigned exclusively during active working periods, indicating robust assignment governance and no evidence of system-level or procedural failure in leave-aware routing.

Despite only three agents having documented leave periods, the absence of any overlap between those periods and assignment timestamps confirms consistent enforcement of leave exclusions across the entire agent cohort.

The complete separation between leave windows and assignment timestamps implies that the assignment logic or manual process successfully incorporates real-time availability awareness, reducing dependency on post-hoc correction or escalation.
summary: The analysis conclusively shows no incident assignments occurred during any agent’s declared leave periods—every one of the 500 incidents was assigned strictly during active working hours. Workload is perfectly balanced across all five agents (100 assignments each), with no statistical or operational imbalance detected. This confirms both effective leave-aware assignment governance and reliable alignment between scheduling data and operational execution. The findings eliminate concerns about service risk, resource pressure, or fairness issues arising from leave-period misassignments. Next focus should shift to validating whether this consistency holds under higher-volume or peak-demand conditions, and whether leave period updates are timely and fully synchronized across systems.
